behaviors.description: All direct and transitive dependencies are extracted from the manifest and included in the vulnerability check.
behaviors.justification: The real failure is a transitive dependency being silently skipped because the lockfile (package-lock.json, poetry.lock, etc.) isn't parsed correctly or completely. A mock resolver would miss format-specific or edge-case resolution failures. Must read real manifest + lockfile files to catch truncation, malformed entries, or incomplete parsing.
behaviors.test_description: Load a project manifest (package.json) and lockfile (package-lock.json) containing both direct and transitive dependencies. Run the vulnerability scan. Verify that all transitive dependencies extracted from the lockfile are included in the vulnerability database queries, not just top-level entries.

behaviors.description: The vulnerability database query returns results that match the exact version ranges in the manifest, not just the package name.
behaviors.justification: The real failure is the code sending only the package name to the database, ignoring version. A mock database could return hardcoded results regardless of what version was sent, masking the bug. Must call the real vulnerability database API (or instrumented mock that validates request parameters) to verify the exact version string is included in the query.
behaviors.test_description: Query the real vulnerability database API for a package with version-specific vulnerabilities (e.g., lodash@4.17.0 vs lodash@4.17.20 have different CVE lists). Verify the request includes the exact version string, and the response is version-specific, not based on package name alone.

behaviors.description: The severity sorting places Critical above High above Medium above Low, with CVEs of the same severity sorted by CVSS score.
behaviors.justification: This is pure comparison logic—no I/O, no external calls. A mock list of vulnerability objects with severity and CVSS fields is sufficient to verify the sorting comparator works correctly. The real failure (wrong sort order) is caught entirely by the sort algorithm.
behaviors.test_description: Run the scanner on a dataset of mock vulnerabilities with mixed severity levels (Critical, High, Medium, Low) and CVSS scores. Verify the output report is sorted first by severity (Critical > High > Medium > Low), then by CVSS score (highest first) within each severity group.

behaviors.description: The manifest modification changes only the affected dependency's version, leaving all other entries unchanged.
behaviors.justification: The real failure is file-format corruption—the code could lose YAML comments, reformat JSON keys, corrupt TOML sections, or accidentally modify other dependencies. Mocking file I/O would hide these issues. Must read and write real manifest files (package.json, requirements.txt, pyproject.toml) to catch format-specific bugs.
behaviors.test_description: Load a real manifest file with multiple dependencies and metadata. Use the scanner's manifest updater to change a single dependency version. Write the file and verify: (a) only the target dependency's version line changed, (b) all other entries remain unchanged, and (c) file formatting (indentation, comments, structure) is preserved.

behaviors.description: The auto-generated pull request is created on the real repository with the correct single-dependency diff.
behaviors.justification: The real failures are: (a) the PR is created with an incorrect diff (includes unrelated changes or misses the version bump), (b) the PR is created on the wrong branch/repository, (c) authentication or GitHub API errors occur. These require real HTTP calls to GitHub (or real git + GitHub integration). A mock GitHub API would accept any request, hiding these issues.
behaviors.test_description: Detect an available dependency update, generate a PR, and create it on a real test repository (or use GitHub API integration test). Verify the PR contains exactly one commit with a single-line diff (only the dependency version change), correct branch, correct title/body, and no unrelated file modifications.

behaviors.description: A major version bump is correctly identified as a breaking change based on semver comparison.
behaviors.justification: This is pure semver logic—given two version strings, determine if the difference is major, minor, or patch. No I/O, no external dependencies. Mock version pairs and the comparison function are sufficient. The real failure (wrong classification) is entirely in the comparison algorithm.
behaviors.test_description: Test the semver comparison function with version pairs: (1.0.0→2.0.0), (1.0.0→1.5.0), (1.0.0→1.0.5). Verify that only major version increments (X.y.z→Y.y.z where Y > X) are classified as breaking changes; minor and patch increments are not.

behaviors.description: When a breaking change is detected, PR creation is skipped and a review flag is surfaced in the scanner output.
behaviors.justification: The real failures are: (a) the code creates a PR despite detecting a breaking change (logic bug), (b) the review flag is not set or is missing from the output (data structure bug). Both are tested with mocks and in-process verification. Mock the GitHub PR API and verify it is not called; verify the returned output object contains the review flag. No I/O or external calls required.
behaviors.test_description: Scan a manifest with a breaking-change update available (e.g., lodash 4.x.x → 5.x.x). Verify: (a) the GitHub PR creation API is not invoked (using a mock to confirm), and (b) the scanner output includes a 'review_required' flag or similar indicator for that dependency.
